title: TPU v1 · 28–40 W Power Architecture: Quantize, Move Less, Remove Overhead
oneLine: Power architecture is not an afterthought; it decides where every joule goes from the outset. TPU uses three levers to minimize energy per MAC, reaching a 28–40 W card TDP far below contemporary GPUs.

legend.name: Lever ① Quantize
legend.desc: Inference tolerates low precision, enabling 8-bit integer multiplication with 32-bit accumulation. Narrow multipliers consume markedly less area and dynamic power than floating-point units.

legend.name: Lever ② Move less data
legend.desc: A systolic array transfers values directly among 65,536 ALUs cycle by cycle with almost no memory access, as the paper notes. Data movement often costs more energy than arithmetic, so removing it attacks the dominant waste.

legend.name: Lever ③ Remove overhead
legend.desc: Eliminate hardware caches, out-of-order machinery, and branch prediction—general-purpose CPU structures that save latency but consume area and power—and dedicate the budget to the MAC array.

legend.name: MACs dominate useful power
legend.desc: The objective is to spend most joules on useful MAC work rather than data movement or control. That allocation is the practical measure of high efficiency.

legend.name: TDP budget
legend.desc: Together, the three levers yield a 28–40 W card TDP. This ceiling in turn limits how many MACs §3.3 can deploy; compute and power are coupled design knobs.

steps.cap: ① Reduce precision: accept INT8 inference, shrink multipliers, and lower energy per MAC.
② Reduce memory traffic: pass data directly between PEs in the systolic array, minimizing data-movement power.
③ Remove control overhead: eliminate caches, out-of-order execution, and branch prediction so area and power serve compute.
④ Result: at equal throughput, most power performs useful work in the MAC array, maximizing energy efficiency.
⑤ Partition power domains using standard practice: manage compute, on-chip memory, and I/O separately while meeting a 28–40 W card TDP.

before: Set the global power budget: How much card TDP, delivery current, and air cooling can a data-center server provide? TPU targets 28–40 W, the hard ceiling for every downstream tradeoff.

Decisions to make: Numerical precision, dataflow and its memory traffic, retention of caches/out-of-order execution/branch prediction, target voltage and frequency, SRAM capacity, and power-domain partitioning.
Required inputs: Workload profile (§3.1), per-operation and per-bit movement energy from the process library, and facility power and cooling limits.
Hard constraint: Data centers optimize TOPS/W, not peak TOPS alone. The power ceiling directly limits MAC count in §3.3; the two must be co-designed.
during: Identify the largest energy cost: In an array accelerator, memory access and long-distance movement often consume more energy than one multiply-accumulate. The first rule is therefore to move less data.

Lever ① trade precision for energy: INT8 inference uses narrower, lower-capacitance multipliers and cuts storage and bandwidth energy by about 4× versus floating point. Use 32-bit accumulation only where needed to control overflow.

Lever ② trade dataflow for fewer accesses: In a weight-stationary systolic flow, weights remain fixed, activations stream, and partial sums move between PEs. Local reuse minimizes memory energy; the paper reports no memory access during transfers among 64K ALUs.

Lever ③ remove structures for density: Caches, out-of-order machinery, and branch predictors reduce latency but consume area and power. Deterministic host-driven inference can omit them and devote the budget to MACs.

Accept the tradeoffs: Replacing caches with a 24 MiB software-managed Unified Buffer makes the compiler responsible for placement and timing. Low precision likewise requires a quantization strategy that preserves accuracy.

Partition power domains carefully: Data-center inference ASICs commonly separate compute, on-chip memory, and I/O for independent voltage/frequency management and clock gating. TPU v1 did not disclose its voltage domains or DVFS implementation.

result.label: Lever ① Quantize
result.value: INT8 multiply / INT32 accumulate; smaller MACs

result.label: Lever ② Move less
result.value: Direct 64K-ALU transfers with almost no memory access

result.label: Power domains (general)
result.value: Compute / on-chip memory / I/O; v1 DVFS not disclosed

after: Validate energy efficiency: Measure TOPS/W and block-level power on real workloads. If movement or control consumes too much, revise the dataflow or on-chip SRAM capacity.
Reconcile with compute: Feed measured power back to §3.3. If over budget, reduce array size or frequency and iterate until both TDP and throughput targets hold.
Propagate constraints: Give physical implementation (⑥) power budgets, domain partitioning, PDN, and thermal requirements; give microarchitecture (④) clock-gating and idle-block shutdown hooks.
Add power observability: Include power/utilization counters and temperature sensors for production TOPS/W monitoring. These details follow standard data-center ASIC practice where v1 is not public.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (undisclosed power-domain and DVFS details are described only as standard data-center inference-ASIC practice.)
